questions.prompt: In the Page Indexing report, you see a URL listed as 'Crawled — currently not indexed.' What does this mean and what's the right fix?

choices.text: It means Googlebot can't reach the page. Fix robots.txt.

It means Googlebot fetched the page successfully, but Google's indexing system chose not to store it — typically a content quality / value signal. The fix is usually content (originality, depth, substance), not technical.
It means the URL has a noindex tag. Remove it.
It means the page is duplicate. Add a canonical.

questions.explanation: 'Crawled — currently not indexed' is the indexing system's way of saying 'we saw it and chose not to store it.' Almost always a content-quality decision: too thin, too duplicate, too templated, too low-value. Submitting again won't change Google's mind. The fix is to make the page substantively worth indexing.

questions.prompt: In the Performance report, you find a query where you have high impressions but very low CTR. What's the most likely fix?

choices.text: Add more keywords to the article body.
Your <title> and/or meta description for that page aren't compelling for users seeing it on the SERP. Rewrite them to be more accurate, clear, and click-worthy for the query.
Add nofollow to all external links.

questions.explanation: High impressions = you're showing up. Low CTR = users see your listing and don't click. The SERP entry — primarily your title link and meta description — is doing the convincing. Rewrite them to be more useful and accurate for the query, and CTR typically responds within weeks.
